title: Curiosity
puzzle: Why does not knowing something sometimes feel almost irresistible?
insight: Curiosity appears when the brain senses a gap between what it knows and what it wants to know. That tension can make learning feel rewarding.
key_ideas: Curiosity often improves attention and memory.
Questions can be more energizing than answers.
mind_blower: One of the brain’s strongest learning tools is the discomfort of an unfinished question.
next_curiosity: If curiosity boosts memory, how should teaching make better use of questions?
narration_text: Why does not knowing something sometimes feel almost irresistible? Curiosity appears when the brain senses a gap between what it knows and what it wants to know. That tension can make learning feel rewarding. Information gaps can create motivation. Curiosity often improves attention and memory. Questions can be more energizing than answers. One of the brain’s strongest learning tools is the discomfort of an unfinished question. If curiosity boosts memory, how should teaching make better use of questions?
